FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study explicitly identified: 'using survey and interview data collected for 9- to 11-year-olds from the 21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'we conducted generalized linear mixed models' with reported coefficients, t-values, and p-values in Table 2

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Child Psychiatry & Human Development (2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Electronic media use operationalized as TV shows/movies, videos, video games, texting, social networks, video chatting (all phone/SM-based activities). Paired with individual-trait outcomes: child anxiety and depression (measured via CBCL)

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports quantitative results: regression coefficients, t-values, p-values, and 95% confidence intervals for EMU predicting anxiety and depression

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric inferential results reported throughout: e.g., child weekday EMU predicting anxiety (t=3.309, p=.001, 95% CI 0.022 to 0.088)

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies where SM is DV-only
notes: Clear phone/SM distinction within composite EMU measure; differential relationships by media type tested

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study explicitly stated: 'cross-sectional baseline data (2016–18) as part of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, a broadly US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'Negative binomial structural equation modeling' with reported indirect and direct effects across multiple pathways

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: ST types isolate phone/SM: television/movies, videos, video games, social media (texting, social networks, video chat). Content includes mature-rated video games and R-rated movies. Paired with individual-trait outcomes: problem behaviors (rule-breaking, aggressive behavior, social problems, thought problems, anxious/depressed, somatic complaints, attention problems, withdrawn/depressed via CBCL)

FT_IC3.evidence: Direct effects reported in Table 2 with IRRs, β, p-values, 95% CIs; e.g., television/movies → rule-breaking (IRR=1.059, p<.001); indirect effects in Table 3 with mediation pathways quantified

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: television/movies associated with 5.9% increase in rule-breaking (IRR=1.059), 5% increase in social problems (IRR=1.050); sleep duration mediated relationships with IRRs ranging from .834 to .905

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies where SM is DV-only
notes: Screen time and content measured separately; sleep duration as mediator; comprehensive problem behavior outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study: 'Participants were part of the Adolescent Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, an ongoing longitudinal, observational study' with 4524 children ages 8-11 years from baseline curated release

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'structural equation modeling' testing associations between movement behavior recommendations and impulsivity dimensions

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is measured as a component of composite adherence to Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines (≤2 hours recreational ST per day) bundled with sleep and physical activity. The methods do not isolate phone or SM as a separately measured construct; rather, ST is part of a multi-domain behavioral composite. No breakdown isolating phone/SM modality from other devices. Criterion 2.4 states: 'Multi-device aggregated time bundling smartphone with non-smartphone devices (minutes on computer + tablet + cellphone) with no per-device breakdown' and 'Composite screen time totals that bundle TV/console/phone with no per-modality breakdown' do NOT qualify. This paper measures ST adherence (yes/no to ≤2 h/day guideline) as a behavioral composite component, not as isolated phone/SM paired with an individual-trait outcome

notes: FT-IC2 fails because ST is not isolated as phone/SM-specific variable; it is bundled as part of composite 24-hour movement guideline adherence without per-device breakdown
